table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Douwe Kiela introduces the concept of multimodality, which involves processing information across multiple modes or types of data (e.g., text, images, audio, olfaction). He explains that multimodality is essential because human comprehension is inherently multimodal (faithfulness), the internet relies on mixed media (practicality), and AI research is depleting available text data, necessitating other data sources to scale models. He highlights the 'McGurk effect' to demonstrate how visual inputs can physically alter auditory perception. The section outlines core multimodal applications, including cross-modal retrieval, image captioning, text-to-image generation (e.g., Stable Diffusion), Visual Question Answering (VQA), and multimodal classification.
key_takeaways: Multimodal deep learning aims to process and synthesize various data types (text, images, audio) to build more human-like, faithful AI models.
Incorporating modalities beyond text is a necessary scaling strategy for foundation models as high-quality textual data becomes scarce.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: This section covers the foundational pre-transformer architectures in vision and language. Kiela discusses early frameworks like WSABI and DeVise, which utilized max-margin loss to align separate vision and language models into a shared multimodal embedding space. This alignment enabled early cross-modal retrieval (e.g., matching the word 'auto' to a picture of a car). He explains 'Bag of Visual Words,' a technique that uses SIFT to extract key points from images, clusters them with k-means to create visual word frequencies, and maps them to visual representations. Kiela details how early deep learning approaches used ConvNets (like AlexNet) for image feature extraction and combined them with word embeddings, or used sequence-to-sequence LSTMs to generate image captions.
key_takeaways: Early multimodal alignment relied on max-margin loss to push similar cross-modal representations closer and dissimilar ones apart in a shared vector space.
Techniques like 'Bag of Visual Words' and early ConvNet-to-LSTM pipelines paved the way for more complex semantic grounding and image captioning.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Kiela details the mechanics of featurizing different data types and the complexities of fusing them. Text is typically represented as a 3D tensor (batch size × sequence length × embedding size). Images are processed either via region features (using object detectors like YOLO to extract bounding boxes of specific objects) or dense features (using Vision Transformers to flatten image patches). The lecture categorizes fusion strategies into early, middle, and late fusion. Kiela highlights various fusion operations, such as inner products, multiplicative gating, and attention mechanisms. He provides the example of FiLM (Feature-wise Linear Modulation), which injects visual or linguistic conditions directly into a ResNet by applying multiplicative (gamma) and additive (beta) modulations at each network layer.
key_takeaways: Image featurization has evolved from extracting specific object bounding boxes (region features) to simply flattening grid patches for Vision Transformers (dense features).
Fusion strategies range from simple late-stage score combinations to complex early/middle fusion techniques like Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) that allow modalities to deeply interact.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The lecture shifts to late fusion architectures, specifically highlighting OpenAI's CLIP and Google's ALIGN. CLIP utilizes a dual-transformer setup (a text transformer and a Vision Transformer) trained on 300 million web-scraped image-text pairs. It uses a simple in-batch contrastive loss to maximize the similarity of correct image-text pairs while minimizing it for incorrect ones. This architecture enables powerful zero-shot label predictions via text prompting (e.g., 'a photo of a cat'). ALIGN scaled this exact concept to 1.8 billion pairs. Kiela also mentions LAION, an open-source dataset boasting up to 5 billion image-text pairs, which serves as the backbone for modern generative models like Stable Diffusion.
key_takeaways: Late fusion models like CLIP prove that massive datasets and high compute, combined with a simple contrastive loss, can yield highly robust, zero-shot capable models.
The availability of massive open-source datasets like LAION has democratized the training of state-of-the-art multimodal foundation models.

table_of_contents.topic_title: Middle and Early Fusion in the BERT Era
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Discussing the explosion of multimodal BERT variants (VisualBERT, ViLBERT, LXMERT, MMBT, ViLT), Kiela explains how researchers integrated visual features into transformer architectures. Models varied by using single-stream (concatenating visual and text tokens into one transformer) or dual-stream (parallel transformers with cross-attention) designs. He notes his own MMBT model, which pooled ResNet features directly into BERT. ViLT marked a shift by abandoning ConvNets entirely, feeding flattened image patches directly into a transformer. Despite the architectural creativity, Kiela cites an ablation study ('Unmasked') revealing that most performance gains came from data scale rather than architectural novelty. He introduces FLAVA, a unified foundation model jointly pretrained on unimodal text, unimodal images, and multimodal pairs to perform diverse tasks within a single architecture.
key_takeaways: The 'BERT era' saw immense architectural experimentation (single-stream vs. dual-stream), but large-scale ablation studies proved that data scale and quality dictate performance more than minor architectural tweaks.
FLAVA represents the transition toward true foundation models capable of handling unimodal and multimodal tasks simultaneously.

table_of_contents.topic_title: Generative Multimodal Models and Frozen LLMs
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Kiela outlines the field's transition from discriminative to generative multimodal models. Early generative attempts like SimVLM added decoders to generate captions. The current state-of-the-art approach involves keeping massive Large Language Models (LLMs like Chinchilla, OPT, or T5) 'frozen' and training lightweight visual encoders or cross-attention layers to project visual data into the LLM's token space. DeepMind's Flamingo uses a Perceiver Resampler to compress visual features from multiple images, applying gated cross-attention before the frozen LLM layers to enable few-shot learning. BLIP-2 further pushed this by freezing almost the entire network. Kiela emphasizes 'Multimodal Chain of Thought' prompting, where models are forced to generate rationales before answering (e.g., in ScienceQA), leading to drastically improved reasoning.
key_takeaways: Freezing massive LLMs and learning to project visual tokens into their embedding space allows models to leverage advanced linguistic reasoning without catastrophic forgetting.
Multimodal Chain of Thought prompting significantly enhances complex visual reasoning and logic tasks.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Evaluating multimodal models is highly challenging. Kiela critiques early datasets like VQA, where models could achieve 70% accuracy on counting questions just by blindly guessing '2', entirely ignoring the image. To counter dataset bias, Kiela created the 'Hateful Memes' dataset using 'benign confounders'—swapping images or text to flip the meme's meaning, forcing models to truly synthesize both modalities. He also discusses the 'Winoground' dataset, which tests compositional understanding by swapping word order (e.g., 'a mug in grass' vs. 'grass in a mug'). State-of-the-art models, including CLIP and DALL-E 2, struggle with Winoground, often performing below random chance, proving that current models act more like statistical pattern matchers than true compositional reasoners.
key_takeaways: Poorly designed benchmarks allow models to exploit statistical biases (e.g., text priors in VQA) rather than learning true multimodal reasoning.
Datasets with 'benign confounders' and strict compositional tests (like Winoground) expose the severe limitations of current state-of-the-art foundation models.

table_of_contents.topic_title: Expanding Modalities: Audio, Video, and Olfaction
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Multimodality extends far beyond images and text. Kiela highlights OpenAI's Whisper, trained on 680,000 hours of speech, which treats audio essentially as vision by feeding log mel spectrograms into a transformer. For video, models like MERLOT Reserve subsample video frames to manage compute and incorporate audio to create trimodal systems. Kiela discusses 'grounded language learning' in simulated 3D environments where AI agents learn by interacting with their surroundings. He also touches on Text-to-3D generation (Point-E). Finally, he presents a novel study on 'olfactory embeddings,' where mapping the chemical compounds of smells (via the Sigma-Aldrich catalog) produced semantic clusters (e.g., apple and pear) that correlated better with human similarity judgments than standard word embeddings.
key_takeaways: Audio and speech can be effectively processed using vision-based techniques by converting audio waveforms into log mel spectrograms.
Grounded, simulated environments and novel modalities like olfaction provide richer, more human-like semantic representations than text alone.

table_of_contents.topic_title: The Future of Multimodal Deep Learning
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: In the concluding section, Kiela predicts the trajectory of multimodal AI. He asserts that the field is moving toward a single, omni-modal foundation model capable of understanding and synthesizing all data types simultaneously. He notes that understanding multimodal 'scaling laws' will be critical to balancing different modalities during training. Furthermore, he anticipates massive growth in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) applied to multimodal contexts. He concludes by reiterating that the biggest hurdle facing the community is the desperate need for rigorous, bias-resistant evaluation benchmarks.
key_takeaways: The ultimate goal of the field is to build unified, modality-agnostic foundation models that mirror human perception.
Future breakthroughs will rely heavily on multimodal scaling laws, retrieval-augmented generation, and significantly improved evaluation metrics.
